paragraphs.text: Dear Commission, I am writing to urge the inclusion of stronger consumer protections in the video game sector within the scope of the Digital Fairness Act. This industry, despite its cultural and economic relevance, routinely engages in practices that exploit consumers particularly minors with little accountability. 1. Arbitrary Game Shutdowns Consumers frequently buy games or in-game content that become unusable when servers are shut down or services are terminated. Even single-player games often require an internet connection, rendering them unplayable post-shutdown. These are not subscriptions, yet access is revoked without refund, notice, or preservation. This violates basic consumer expectations of ownership and longevity. 2. Gambling-like Monetization Loot boxes and gacha systems use variable-ratio rewards the same techniques seen in gambling often targeting minors. These mechanics obscure real-world costs through virtual currencies and exploit psychological vulnerabilities. Odds are rarely transparent, and spending caps are nonexistent. Some Member States have recognized the gambling risks, but there is no consistent EU-level action. 3. Addictive Design & Dark Patterns Games increasingly rely on addictive mechanics time-limited rewards, infinite progression loops, fear-of-missing-out to keep players engaged and spending. Monetization is deeply embedded in gameplay and built on behavioral manipulation, especially harmful to children. Players are nudged into poor financial decisions without full understanding or control. 4. Unclear Rights and Poor Recourse Platforms have inconsistent refund and cancellation policies. Free trials convert to paid subscriptions with minimal notice. Automated customer service often blocks fair redress. Consumers lack real avenues to resolve disputes or reclaim losses, especially in cross-border digital transactions. What the Digital Fairness Act Should Address: Mandatory end-of-life policies and offline access for games. Ban on selling gambling mechanics to minors. Full transparency in digital purchases and spending. Limits on addictive design and psychological exploitation. Harmonized refund, cancellation, and complaint standards across platforms. The video game industry should no longer be treated as an exception. It is one of the clearest examples of digital unfairness in the market today. Strong regulation is essential to protect consumers especially young ones from exploitation masked as entertainment. Sincerely, A concerned EU Citizen
